claims.text: A document calling itself 'Project Anchor: Gravitational Discontinuity Event Executive Summary' began circulating on encrypted channels in December 2025, claiming NASA has known since 2019 that converging gravitational waves from a billion-light-year-distant black-hole merger will briefly cancel Earth's gravity for 7 seconds at 14:33 UTC on August 12, 2026, with 94.7% probability and an $89 billion mitigation budget.

evidence.quote:
In December 2025, a document started
circulating on encrypted channels. The
header read project anchor gravitational
discontinuity event executive summary.
[music] The document was marked
classified at a level above top secret.
It described a global emergency program
with an $89 billion budget and one
terrifying purpose. Preparing humanity
for a moment when Earth's gravity would
temporarily cease [music] to exist. The
date was precise. August 12th, 2026,
1433 UTC. For about 7 seconds, the
gravitational force holding everything
to the planet would drop to zero. And
according to the document, NASA
identified this threat in 2019. Deep
space monitoring stations detected two
gravitational wave signatures
converging. These waves come from
merging black holes a billion lighty
years away. And when they intersect near
Earth's orbital position in August 2026,
the interference pattern will briefly
cancel out our planet's gravitational
field.
The probability assessment in the
document was 94.7%. [music]
That's not speculation, that's a
certainty.
>> [music]
>> Dr. Thomas Webb noticed the anomaly at
2:47 a.m. LIGO had de

claims.rationale: It is true that a 'Project Anchor' document went viral on TikTok and Telegram in late 2025; the host accurately reports the document's content. The document itself is verifiably fictional - the host's own verdict explicitly debunks it as a hoax. Gravitational waves do not work as the document describes (they are spacetime ripples, not interfering force beams), and NASA has confirmed no such event will occur.

claims.text: Newton's 1687 law of universal gravitation describes how gravity behaves perfectly but offers no mechanism for why it works; Einstein's 1915 general relativity reframed gravity as the curvature of spacetime by mass, and his equations correctly predict planetary motion, gravitational lensing, and time dilation in GPS satellites - but still don't say what gravity *is*.

evidence.quote:
Every physicist in history has tried to
answer the same question. What is
gravity? Isaac Newton figured out how it
behaves in 1687. He published the laws
of universal gravitation. Every object
with mass attracts every other object
with mass. The bigger the mass, the
stronger the pull. The farther apart,
the weaker the pull. Simple math. It
works for planets, cannonballs, and
feathers. [music] But Newton had no idea
why it worked. He admitted this freely.
Gravity was an invisible force acting
across empty space with no mechanism to
explain it. He said he'd leave the
explanation to future generations. That
was over 300 years ago. Albert Einstein
took the next step in 1915. His general
theory of relativity redefined gravity.
Mass warps spaceime. That warping or
bending of spaceime is gravity. Think of
a bowling ball on a trampoline. The ball
creates a depression. Anything rolling
nearby curves toward it, not because the
ball is pulling, because the surface is
bent. Planets orbit the sun because the
sun's mass has curved spaceime into a
shape where orbiting is the natural
path. They're just following the
geometry. Einstein's equations work
p

claims.rationale: This is mainstream undergraduate physics. Newton's Principia (1687), Einstein's general relativity (1915), the experimental confirmations (Eddington 1919, GPS time-dilation corrections), and the philosophical observation that 'how' is well-known but 'why/what' is not - all standard.

claims.text: Gravity remains physics' biggest mystery: its hypothesized carrier particle (the graviton) has never been detected and per Freeman Dyson may be undetectable in principle, it is roughly 10^40 times weaker than electromagnetism (the 'hierarchy problem'), and it varies measurably across Earth's surface (Hudson Bay, the Indian Ocean geoid low) - leaving open the question of whether it could be artificially manipulated.

evidence.quote:
Every force in nature has a carrier
particle. Electromagnetism uses photons.
The strong nuclear force uses gluons.
The weak force uses W and Z bzons. These
particles physically carry the force
from one place to another. Gravity
should have a carrier particle, too.
Physicists call it the graviton. But the
graviton has never been detected, not
even once. Freeman Dyson calculated that
equipment sensitive enough to find a
single graviton would need to be so
massive it would collapse into a black
hole before it could make a measurement.
Then there's another mystery. Gravity is
weak. Extremely weak. It's 10 to the
40th power weaker than electromagnetism.
That's a one with 40 zeros after it.
Want to see how weak gravity is? Pick up
a paperclip with a small magnet. That
tiny magnet is overpowering the
gravitational pull of the entire Earth.
Physicists call this the hierarchy
problem. Why is gravity so different
from other forces? We don't know. What
we do know is that gravity varies
slightly across Earth's surface. Hudson
Bay in Canada has a gravitational
anomaly. Objects there weigh about
0.004%
less than normal.
>> Less gravity in Canada, huh? That
explains curling. They're basically
cheating.
>> The Indian Ocean has a ge

claims.rationale: All four facts are accurate mainstream physics: the graviton is hypothesized but undetected; Dyson's argument about graviton-detection requiring black-hole-scale apparatus is from his 2013 paper; gravity is indeed ~10^40 weaker than EM (the hierarchy problem); the Hudson Bay negative gravity anomaly and Indian Ocean geoid low are well-mapped via GRACE satellite data.

claims.text: Thomas Townsend Brown's 1920s 'electrogravitics' research - claiming high-voltage electrical fields could couple with gravity to produce thrust without exhaust - attracted serious 1950s investment from Douglas, Glenn Martin, GE, Bell, Convair, Lear, and other aerospace primes; then in 1956 the entire field went dark and never resurfaced in the public literature, with Brown continuing only on classified contracts until his 1985 death.

evidence.quote:
Thomas Townsen Brown spent his 16th
birthday in his parents' basement
watching an X-ray tube hover. Not float,
hover like something was supporting it.
Most kids wanted cars. Brown wanted to
break physics.
The tube was supposed to just produce
X-rays. But Brown noticed something
strange when he applied a high voltage
to certain materials. They moved. Not
much, just a tiny thrust, but thrust
without fuel, without exhaust, without
any visible means of propulsion. He
spent the next decade refining the
effect. He called it electrogravidics.
The idea was simple. High voltage
electrical fields could somehow couple
with gravity, producing thrust toward
the positive electrode. By the 1950s,
Brown had caught the attention of the US
military. Classified reports from 1956
show that major aerospace companies were
investigating his work. Douglas
Aircraft, Glenn Martin, General
Electric, Bell Aircraft, Convey, Lear,
Spirand, all of them had
electrogravidics programs. An Aviation
Week article from that year reported
that the industry was treating gravity
control as

claims.rationale: Thomas Townsend Brown is a real figure (1905-1985) and the Aviation Week 1956 article 'Conquest of Gravity Aim of Top Scientists' is real - it did list several aerospace companies investigating gravity-control. However, the modern consensus is that Brown's effect (the Biefeld-Brown effect) is just ion-wind thrust through air, not actual gravity manipulation. The 'classified disappearance' framing is partly true (later work moved into industry secrecy) but partly an overread - mainstream physics simply concluded the effect was mundane.

claims.text: Russian physicist Eugene Podkletnov claimed in 1992 that a rotating superconducting disc could reduce the weight of objects above it by ~2%; his paper was retracted under pressure, his coauthor withdrew, multiple labs failed to replicate, and by 2001 he was describing an 'impulse gravity generator' capable of knocking down objects from a mile away.

evidence.quote:
In 1992, Russian physicist Eugene
Padletnovv made headlines. He claimed
that a rotating superconducting disc
could reduce the weight of objects above
it by about 2%. The paper was submitted
to a peer-reviewed journal. His
co-author saw the data and signed off.
Then the story broke in the Sunday
[music] Telegraph.
The headline read, "World's first
anti-gravity device." The university
panicked. Pletenov's co-author withdrew
his name. The paper was pulled. Pletenov
lost his position. [music]
By 2001, he was describing an impulse
gravity generator that could knock down
objects from a mile away. No missile, no
projectile, a beam of gravitational
force. Now, multiple institutions tried
to replicate his results, but none
succeeded. But Pletenov's equipment
worked and nobody can explain why. Then
there's the case of Dr. Ning Lee. She
was a Chinese American physicist at the
University of Alabama in Huntsville. In
the 1990s, she published papers on
rotating ions in superconductors that
created gravidometric fields. NASA
funded her early research. In 2001, she
received a $450,000
grant from the Department of Defense.
Then she founded a company called AC
Gravity LLC and she obtained top secret
security clearance. But after 2002, Dr.
Ning Lee stopped publishing. No papers,
no conferences, no public statements.
When journalists tried to contact her,
she refused to speak. Then she
disappeared. Literally, nobody knew
where she went. But 12 years later, she
was back.
But soon after returning, she was hit by
a car that caused permanent brain
damage. She died in July

claims.rationale: Yevgeny Podkletnov is a real Russian physicist; his 1992 Tampere University claim and the subsequent retraction/coauthor withdrawal are real. NASA's MSFC actually attempted replication in the late 1990s and failed. The 'multiple labs failed to replicate but his equipment worked' framing is misleading - failed replications are exactly why the original claim is generally not believed. The 2001 'impulse gravity generator' claim was published in arxiv but never independently confirmed.

claims.text: Ning Li, a Chinese-American physicist at the University of Alabama Huntsville, published in the 1990s on superconductor-rotating-ion gravidometric effects, received a $450K DoD grant in 2001, founded AC Gravity LLC, obtained top-secret clearance, then vanished from public view from 2002 to 2014; she returned briefly, was injured by a car causing brain damage, and died in July 2021 - her research never released.

evidence.quote:

Padletnovv made headlines. He claimed
that a rotating superconducting disc
could reduce the weight of objects above

claims.rationale: Dr. Ning Li is a real, well-documented figure: UAH professor in the 1990s, AC Gravity LLC, DoD funding, and a long publishing gap. She did die in 2021. The interpretation that her work was absorbed into a classified anti-gravity program is speculation but not unreasonable given her trajectory. The 'hit by a car' detail is from secondary reporting and is plausible but I haven't independently confirmed it.

claims.text: Bob Lazar's 1989 claim that he reverse-engineered alien craft at S-4 near Area 51 - propelled by gravity-warping element 115 - was widely dismissed as fraud, but element 115 (Moscovium) was synthesized in 2003, and 2004 Navy pilots off the USS Nimitz observed objects performing the exact spacetime-warping maneuvers Lazar described.

evidence.quote:
In 1989, Bob Lazar went on Las Vegas
television and claimed he'd worked at a
facility called S4 [music] near Area 51
in the Nevada desert.
He said he helped reverse engineer alien
spacecraft. Lazar said the propulsion
system used element 115 to generate
gravity awes. These waves could bend
[music] spaceime creating the effect of
falling toward a target. No rockets, no
combustion, just gravitational
manipulation. Lazar was dismissed as a
fraud. There was no such thing as
element [music] 115 until there was. It
was synthesized in 2003 and named
Muscovium.
>> Wait, wait, wait, wait. Lazar said there
was an element that didn't exist and
then 14 years later it did.
>> Yes, but their version decays in a
fraction of a second.
>> Their version. It's alien technology.
They're doing it wrong.
>> Yeah, that's not how that works.
>> If I built a car, it exploded every time
I turned it on. I wouldn't say cars
don't work. I'd say I'm a bad mechanic.
>> Believe Lazar or don't.
>> Believe. But in 2004, Navy pilots off
the USS Nimmitz [music] encountered an
object that moved exactly the way Lazar
described.
It dropped from 80,000 ft to sea level
in less than a second. Over 12,000 G
forces. No human pilot could survive
that. [music] No conventional aircraft
could either. The object had no wings,
no exhaust, no heat signature. It
traveled at supersonic

claims.rationale: Lazar's 1989 KLAS interview, his element-115 claim, the 2003 synthesis of Moscovium, and the 2004 USS Nimitz tic-tac UAP encounter are all real. The implied connection - that Lazar predicted Moscovium and the Nimitz UAP confirms his propulsion mechanism - is the host's leap. Moscovium has a half-life of ~1 second and cannot store energy or generate stable gravity fields; Lazar's claimed stable isotope still doesn't exist. The Nimitz encounter is real but the object's propulsion is unknown - Lazar's mechanism is one of many speculative explanations.

claims.text: In the host's verdict, Project Anchor is definitively a hoax: there is no $89 billion program, no 94.7% probability calculation, and gravitational waves don't physically work the way the document describes - NASA itself was forced to issue a public statement debunking it.

evidence.quote:
Project Anchor is zooming around the
internet, terrifying everybody. And
today we saw why. Losing gravity, even
for a few seconds, would be a disaster.
So, should you be afraid? Should you
start buying ceiling anchors and cables?
No. Project Anchor is a hoax. The leaked
document first appeared on Tik Tok and
Instagram in late December 2025, and
it's pure fiction. There's no $89
billion program. There's no 94.7
probability calculation. NASA has never
monitored gravitational waves that could
intersect near Earth. That's not how
gravitational waves even work. They're
ripples in spaceime itself, not beams
that can collide. But the story went so
viral that NASA was forced to issue a
public statement.
>> Earth will not lose gravity for 7
seconds on August 12th, 2026, or any
other time for that matter. The only way
Earth could suddenly lose its gravity
would be if the planet suddenly lost its
mass.
>> How did NASA Pinky promise? Because
their track record uh isn't great.
>> Yeah, fair enough. But I think NASA's
right about this one.
>> Human, if you believe NASA's press
releases, I got a moon landing to sell
you.
>> The date was chosen because there's a
real total solar eclipse that day.
Doomsday hoaxes have always attached
themselves to eclipses, and this one is
no different. But here's where things
get interesting. We genuinely don't know
what gravity is. Einstein's

claims.rationale: The host's debunking is correct on the physics: gravitational waves are spacetime metric perturbations, not interfering force beams; their amplitudes near Earth are 10^-21 strain at best, vastly too weak to nullify Earth's gravity. NASA did publicly debunk Project Anchor. The eclipse-as-doomsday-trigger is a known meme pattern.

claims.text: While Project Anchor itself is a hoax, the host argues the broader pattern is real and damning: anti-gravity research has been classified for 70+ years (Brown, Podkletnov, Ning Li, Lazar/element 115), and the only coherent explanation for that secrecy is that working anti-gravity exists - because it would simultaneously be the most powerful weapons platform ever conceived AND the end of the trillion-dollar fossil-energy economy.

evidence.quote:
But if they
found nothing, why classified nothing
for 70 years? Why not just say it
doesn't work and release the research?
Because if anti-gravity works, it's the
most powerful weapons platform ever
conceived. Silent propulsion, instant
acceleration, payloads delivered
anywhere on Earth without detection. The
country that cracks anti-gravity doesn't
just win wars. It makes war obsolete.
Well, obsolete for everyone else. And
that's worth killing for. And that's
worth hiding forever. And if it also
leads to free energy, 0 point vacuum
extraction, whatever you want to call
it. You're not just talking about
military advantage. You're talking about
the collapse of the energy economy.
Trillions of dollars a year gone. The
people who control that money have
direct lines to the intelligence
agencies. They don't want any
disruption. Scientists who cooperate get
absorbed into classified programs.
Scientists who don't, they get
discredited or they have accidents.
Project Ankor isn't real, but
anti-gravity research is. [music] But
it's going to stay secret. If free
energy and anti-gravity technology were
available to everyone on Earth, our
entire species would take a great leap
forward. With this technology, poor
nations could easily be modernized.
Countries that depend on foreign energy
could be self-sufficient and
independent. Wars for energy, which is

claims.rationale: The 'why classify nothing?' argument is a real rhetorical move but logically weak - things get classified for many reasons (ongoing investigation, prevent foreign exploitation of dead-end leads, cover institutional embarrassment) without implying the underlying tech works. That said, the host correctly notes anti-gravity research IS classified, and the strategic and economic stakes if it ever did work would be civilizational. This is a defensible 'someone is hiding something' inference, not a proof.
